title: THE INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY ACT, 2000
summary: The Information Technology Act establishes an extraterritorial legal framework that grants functional equivalence to electronic records and signatures, facilitating electronic governance, digital contracts, and the legal validity of electronic transactions. It regulates the technical infrastructure of digital commerce by overseeing the licensing of Certifying Authorities, mandating security protocols for asymmetric cryptography, and defining the statutory duties of subscribers regarding private key management. The Act imposes a dual liability regime, prescribing civil compensation for unauthorized access and data negligence via Adjudicating Officers, while defining a criminal framework for offences such as identity theft, cyber terrorism, and tampering with computer source documents. This structure is supported by a specialized adjudicatory and evidentiary system involving the TDSAT appellate mechanism, the appointment of Examiners of Electronic Evidence, and the authority of the Central Government to intercept or block information to safeguard national sovereignty.

children.title: Front Matter (Arrangement of Sections & Preamble)
children.summary: The provided section, Front Matter (Arrangement of Sections & Preamble), contains no substantive content. There are no legal concepts, offences, actors, mechanisms, numerical thresholds, or punishments specified within this section.

children.title: CHAPTER 1: P RELIMINARY
children.summary: Chapter 1 (Preliminary) establishes the extraterritorial jurisdiction and application of the Information Technology Act, 2000, across India, providing foundational legal recognition to electronic records and electronic signatures. It defines critical technical infrastructure and legal actors, including computer resources, asymmetric crypto systems, intermediaries, Certifying Authorities, and the Appellate Tribunal. The chapter further mandates authentication standards via hash functions and reliable electronic signatures to ensure the legal validity of digital transactions and electronic records.

children.title: 1. Short title, extent, commencement and application
children.summary: The Information Technology Act, 2000, extends to the whole of India and applies to any offence or contravention committed outside India by any person, save as otherwise provided. It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification, appoint, allowing different dates for different provisions, where any reference to the commencement of this Act shall be construed as a reference to the commencement of that provision. An exception exists for documents or transactions specified in the First Schedule, subject to a Proviso that the Central Government may amend the First Schedule via notification in the Official Gazette by way of addition or deletion of entries, and a requirement that every such notification shall be laid before each House of Parliament.
children.content: 1. Short title, extent, commencement and application.– ( 1 ) This Act may be called the Information Technology Act, 2000. ( 2 ) It shall extend to the whole of India and, save as otherwise provided in this Act, it applies also to any offence or contravention thereunder committed outside India by any person. ( 3 ) It shall come into force on such date 1 as the Central Government may, by notification, appoint and different dates may be appointed for different provisions of this Act and any reference in any such provision to the commencement of this Act shall be construed as a reference to the commencement of that provision. ( 4 ) Nothing in this Act shall apply to documents or transactions specified in the First Schedule: Provided that the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, amend the First Schedule by way of addition or deletion of entries thereto. ( 5 ) Every notification issued under sub-section ( 4 ) shall be laid before each House of Parliament.]

children.title: 2. Definitions
children.summary: Section 2 defines technical infrastructure including "computer", "computer system" (excluding non-programmable calculators), "computer network", "computer resource", "communication device", and "cyber cafe". It specifies legal actors such as the "adjudicating officer" (appointed under sub-section (1) of section 46), "Appellate Tribunal" (sub-section (1) of section 48), "Controller" (appointed under sub-section (1) of section 17), "Certifying Authority" (licensed under section 24), and "appropriate Government" (defined via List II and List III of the Seventh Schedule). Finally, it establishes mechanisms for "access" to logical, arithmetical, or memory function resources, "affixing electronic signature", "asymmetric crypto system" (consisting of a private key and a public key), "certification practice statement", and "addressee" (explicitly excluding any intermediary).

children.title: 5. Clause ( n ) omitted by 7 of 2017, s.169 (w.e.f. 26
children.summary: This section defines terms including "cyber security", "data", "digital signature", "electronic signature", "intermediary", "originator", and "subscriber", and Section 3 allows subscribers to authenticate electronic records via an asymmetric crypto system and hash function, with an Explanation defining "hash function" such that it is computationally infeasible to reconstruct the original electronic record or produce identical hash results from different records. Section 3A permits authentication via reliable electronic signatures or techniques specified in the Second Schedule, subject to a Proviso that no technique is specified unless it is reliable and a requirement that all such Central Government notifications be laid before each House of Parliament. Sections 4 and 5 provide legal recognition for electronic records and electronic signatures, respectively, with Section 5 including an Explanation that "signed" and "signature" include affixing a handwritten signature or any mark.

( 2 ) Any reference in this Act to any enactment or any provision thereof shall, in relation to an area in which such enactment or such provision is not in force, be construed as a reference to the corresponding law or the relevant provision of the corresponding law, if any, in force in that area. IGITAL SIGNATURE AND ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE 3. Authentication of electronic records.– ( 1 ) Subject to the provisions of this section any subscriber may authenticate an electronic record by affixing his digital signature. ( 2 ) The authentication of the electronic record shall be effected by the use of asymmetric crypto system and hash function which envelop and transform the initial electronic record into another electronic record. Explanation .–For the purposes of this sub-section, “hash function” means an algorithm mapping or translation of one sequence of bits into another, generally smaller, set known as “hash result” such that an electronic record yields the same hash result every time the algorithm is executed with the same electronic record as its input making it computationally infeasible– ( a ) to derive or reconstruct the original electronic record from the hash result produced by the algorithm; ( b ) that two electronic records can produce the same hash result using the algorithm. ( 3 ) Any person by the use of a public key of the subscriber can verify the electronic record. ( 4 ) The private key and the public key are unique to the subscriber and constitute a functioning key pair. 3A. Electronic signature. –( 1 ) Notwithstanding anything contained in section 3, but subject to the provisions of sub-section ( 2 ), a subscriber may authenticate any electronic record by such electronic signature or electronic authentication technique which– ( a ) is considered reliable; and ( b ) may be specified in the Second Schedule. ( 5 ) Every notification issued under sub-section ( 4 ) shall be laid before each House of Parliament.] 4. Legal recognition of electronic records.– Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be in writing or in the typewritten or printed form, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such information or matter is– ( a ) rendered or made available in an electronic form; and ( b ) accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference. 5. Legal recognition of 1 [electronic signatures]. –Where any law provides that information or any other matter shall be authenticated by affixing the signature or any document shall be signed or bear the signature of any person, then, notwithstanding anything contained in such law, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied, if such information or matter is authenticated by means of 1 [electronic signature] affixed in such manner as may be prescribed by the Central Government. Explanation .–For the purposes of this section, “signed”, with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, shall, with reference to a person, mean affixing of his hand written signature or any mark on any document and the expression “signature” shall be construed accordingly.

children.title: CHAPTER III: E LECTRONIC GOVERNANCE
children.summary: Chapter III establishes the legal framework for **electronic governance** by granting functional equivalence to **electronic records** and **electronic signatures** for government functions, including filing, licensing, and monetary transactions. It regulates the **retention of electronic records**, validates publication via **Electronic Gazette**, and empowers the Central Government to prescribe security protocols for **electronic signatures** and ensure the enforceability of **electronic contracts**. Notably, the scope is limited by provisions that prevent these rules from conferring a mandatory legal right to insist that government authorities accept or process documents and transactions in electronic form.

children.title: 6. Use of electronic records and 1 [electronic signatures] in Government and its agencies
children.summary: Section 6 stipulates that requirements for filing any form, application, or document; issuing/granting any licence, permit, sanction, or approval; or the receipt/payment of money with any office, authority, body, or agency owned or controlled by the appropriate Government are deemed satisfied if effected via prescribed electronic records and electronic signatures, with the appropriate Government prescribing the manner, format, and method of payment via rules. Section 6A authorizes the appropriate Government to, via Official Gazette notification, permit service providers—defined by an Explanation to include any individual, private agency, private company, partnership firm, sole proprietor firm, or other body or agency—to set up, maintain, and upgrade computerized facilities. Such providers may collect, retain, and appropriate prescribed service charges notwithstanding the absence of express provisions in the governing Act, rule, regulation, or notification, subject to the appropriate Government specifying scales in the Official Gazette, with a Proviso allowing different scales of service charges for different types of services.
children.content: 6. Use of electronic records and 1 [electronic signatures] in Government and its agencies. –( 1 ) Where any law provides for– ( a ) the filing of any form, application or any other document with any office, authority, body or agency owned or controlled by the appropriate Government in a particular manner; ( b ) the issue or grant of any licence, permit, sanction or approval by whatever name called in a particular manner; ( c ) the receipt or payment of money in a particular manner, then, notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such filing, issue, grant, receipt or payment, as the case may be, is effected by means of such electronic form as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government. ( 2 ) The appropriate Government may, for the purposes of sub-section ( 1 ), by rules, prescribe– ( a ) the manner and format in which such electronic records shall be filed, created or issued; 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signatures” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). ( b ) the manner or method of payment of any fee or charges for filing, creation or issue any electronic record under clause ( a ).

children.title: 6A. Delivery of services by service provider
children.summary: The appropriate Government may, via order and notification in the Official Gazette, authorize any service provider to set up, maintain and upgrade computerized facilities, where the Explanation defines such provider to include any individual, private agency, private company, partnership firm, sole proprietor firm or other body/agency granted permission to offer services through electronic means. Subject to sub-section (2), the appropriate Government may authorize these providers to collect, retain and appropriate prescribed service charges, notwithstanding the absence of express provisions under any Act, rule, regulation or notification to collect, retain and appropriate e-service charges. The appropriate Government shall specify the scale of service charges via notification in the Official Gazette, subject to a Proviso that the appropriate Government may specify different scale of service charges for different types of services.
children.content: 6A. Delivery of services by service provider. –( 1 ) The appropriate Government may, for the purposes of this Chapter and for efficient delivery of services to the public through electronic means authorise, by order, any service provider to set up, maintain and upgrade the computerised facilities and perform such other services as it may specify, by notification in the Official Gazette. Explanation .–For the purposes of this section, service provider so authorised includes any individual, private agency, private company, partnership firm, sole proprietor firm or any such other body or agency which has been granted permission by the appropriate Government to offer services through electronic means in accordance with the policy governing such service sector. ( 2 ) The appropriate Government may also authorise any service provider authorised under sub- section ( 1 ) to collect, retain and appropriate such service charges, as may be prescribed by the appropriate Government for the purpose of providing such services, from the person availing such service. ( 3 ) Subject to the provisions of sub-section ( 2 ), the appropriate Government may authorise the service providers to collect, retain and appropriate service charges under this section notwithstanding the fact that there is no express provision under the Act, rule, regulation or notification under which the service is provided to collect, retain and appropriate e-service charges by the service providers. ( 4 ) The appropriate Government shall, by notification in the Official Gazette, specify the scale of service charges which may be charged and collected by the service providers under this section: Provided that the appropriate Government may specify different scale of service charges for different types of services.]

children.title: 7. Retention of electronic records
children.summary: Section 7(1) deems requirements for laws providing that documents, records or information shall be retained for any specific period satisfied if retained in electronic form, provided the information remains accessible for subsequent reference, is in its original or an accurate format, and contains details facilitating the identification of the origin, destination, date and time of despatch or receipt. A proviso to clause (c) excludes information automatically generated solely for the purpose of enabling an electronic record to be despatched or received, and Section 7(2) excludes application to any law that expressly provides for the retention of documents, records or information in the form of electronic records. Section 7A stipulates that where any law for the time being in force provides for audit of documents, records or information, that provision shall also be applicable for audit of documents, records or information processed and maintained in the electronic form.
children.content: 7. Retention of electronic records. –( 1 ) Where any law provides that documents, records or information shall be retained for any specific period, then, that requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such documents, records or information are retained in the electronic form, if– ( a ) the information contained therein remains accessible so as to be usable for a subsequent reference; ( b ) the electronic record is retained in the format in which it was originally generated, sent or received or in a format which can be demonstrated to represent accurately the information originally generated, sent or received; ( c ) the details which will facilitate the identification of the origin, destination, date and time of despatch or receipt of such electronic record are available in the electronic record: Provided that this clause does not apply to any information which is automatically generated solely for the purpose of enabling an electronic record to be despatched or received. ( 2 ) Nothing in this section shall apply to any law that expressly provides for the retention of documents, records or information in the form of electronic records.  7A. Audit of documents, etc., maintained in electronic form.– Where in any law for the time being in force, there is a provision for audit of documents, records or information, that provision shall also be applicable for audit of documents, records or information processed and maintained in the electronic form.]

children.title: 8. Publication of rule, regulation, etc., in Electronic Gazette
children.summary: Section 8 provides that where any law requires a rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification, or any other matter to be published in the Official Gazette, such requirement is deemed satisfied if published in the Official Gazette or Electronic Gazette. The section includes a Proviso stating that the date of publication shall be deemed to be the date of the Gazette which was first published in any form.
children.content: 8. Publication of rule, regulation, etc., in Electronic Gazette. –Where any law provides that any rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter shall be published in the Official Gazette, then, such requirement shall be deemed to have been satisfied if such rule, regulation, order, bye-law, notification or any other matter is published in the Official Gazette or Electronic Gazette: Provided that where any rule, regulation, order, by-law, notification or any other matter is published in the Official Gazette or Electronic Gazette, the date of publication shall be deemed to be the date of the Gazette which was first published in any form.

children.title: 9. Sections 6, 7 and 8 not to confer right to insist document should be accepted in electronic form
children.summary: Section 9 stipulates that nothing contained in Sections 6, 7 and 8 shall confer a right upon any person to insist that any Ministry or Department of the Central Government or the State Government, or any authority or body established by or under any law or controlled or funded by the Central or State Government, should accept, issue, create, retain and preserve any document in the form of electronic records. Furthermore, no such right is conferred to insist that these entities effect any monetary transaction in the electronic form.
children.content: 9. Sections 6, 7 and 8 not to confer right to insist document should be accepted in electronic form.– Nothing contained in sections 6, 7 and 8 shall confer a right upon any person to insist that any 1. Ministry or Department of the Central Government or the State Government or any authority or body established by or under any law or controlled or funded by the Central or State Government should accept, issue, create, retain and preserve any document in the form of electronic records or effect any monetary transaction in the electronic form.

children.title: 10. Power to make rules by Central Government in respect of 1 [electronic signature]
children.summary: The Central Government is empowered to prescribe rules regarding "electronic signature," specifically its type, manner and format of affixing, identification procedures for the person affixing it, and control processes to ensure integrity, security, and confidentiality of electronic records or payments. Under Section 10A, contracts where the communication, acceptance, and revocation of proposals and acceptances are expressed in electronic form or by means of electronic records shall not be deemed unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used.
children.content: 10. Power to make rules by Central Government in respect of 1 [electronic signature]. –The Central Government may, for the purposes of this Act, by rules, prescribe– ( a ) the type of 1 [electronic signature]; ( b ) the manner and format in which the 1 [electronic signature] shall be affixed; ( c ) the manner or procedure which facilitates identification of the person affixing the 1 [electronic signature]; ( d ) control processes and procedures to ensure adequate integrity, security and confidentiality of electronic records or payments; and ( e ) any other matter which is necessary to give legal effect to 1 [electronic signatures].

children.title: 10A. Validity of contracts formed through electronic means
children.summary: Section 10A affirms the validity of contracts formed through electronic means. It mandates that where the communication of proposals, acceptance of proposals, and revocation of proposals and acceptances are expressed in electronic form or by means of electronic records, such contracts shall not be deemed unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used for that purpose.
children.content: 10A. Validity of contracts formed through electronic means. –Where in a contract formation, the communication of proposals, the acceptance of proposals, the revocation of proposals and acceptances, as the case may be, are expressed in electronic form or by means of an electronic records, such contract shall not be deemed to be unenforceable solely on the ground that such electronic form or means was used for that purpose.]

children.title: CHAPTER IV: A TTRIBUTION, ACKNOWLEDGMENT AND DESPATCH OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS
children.summary: Chapter IV establishes the legal framework for the attribution, acknowledgment, and despatch of electronic records, defining the conditions under which a record is attributed to an originator via personal action, authorized agency, or automated information systems. It regulates the modalities and binding nature of acknowledgment of receipt and prescribes the legal time and place of despatch and receipt based on the entry of records into computer resources and the parties' designated places of business.

children.title: 11. Attribution of electronic records
children.summary: Section 11 dictates that an electronic record shall be attributed to the originator if it was sent by the originator himself or by a person who had the authority to act on behalf of the originator in respect of that electronic record. Additionally, attribution occurs if the electronic record is sent by an information system programmed by or on behalf of the originator to operate automatically.
children.content: 11. Attribution of electronic records. –An electronic record shall be attributed to the originator– ( a ) if it was sent by the originator himself; ( b ) by a person who had the authority to act on behalf of the originator in respect of that electronic record; or ( c ) by an information system programmed by or on behalf of the originator to operate automatically.

children.title: 12. Acknowledgment of receipt
children.summary: Under Section 12(1), if the originator has not stipulated a particular form or method for acknowledgment of receipt of an electronic record, acknowledgment may be given via any communication (automated or otherwise) or conduct of the addressee sufficient to indicate receipt. Per Section 12(2), if the originator stipulates that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of an acknowledgment, the electronic record shall be deemed to have been never sent unless such acknowledgment is received. Under Section 12(3), where bindingness is not stipulated and acknowledgment is not received within the specified, agreed, or reasonable time, the originator may give notice specifying a reasonable time, after which they may treat the electronic record as though it has never been sent.
children.content: 12. Acknowledgment of receipt. –( 1 ) Where the originator has not 3 [stipulated] that the acknowledgment of receipt of electronic record be given in a particular form or by a particular method, an acknowledgment may be given by– ( a ) any communication by the addressee, automated or otherwise; or ( b ) any conduct of the addressee, sufficient to indicate to the originator that the electronic record has been received. ( 2 ) Where the originator has stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of an acknowledgment of such electronic record by him, then unless acknowledgment has been so received, the electronic record shall he deemed to have been never sent by the originator. ( 3 ) Where the originator has not stipulated that the electronic record shall be binding only on receipt of such acknowledgment, and the acknowledgment has not been received by the originator within the time specified or agreed or, if no time has been specified or agreed to within a reasonable time, then the originator may give notice to the addressee stating that no acknowledgment has been received by him and specifying a reasonable time by which the acknowledgement must be received by him and if no acknowledgment is received within the aforesaid time limit he may after giving notice to the addressee, treat the electronic record as though it has never been sent.

children.title: 13. Time and place of despatch and receipt of electronic record
children.summary: Save as otherwise agreed between the originator and the addressee, despatch occurs when an electronic record enters a computer resource outside the control of the originator and is deemed to be despatched at the originator's place of business. Receipt is deemed to occur at the addressee's place of business, with timing determined by: entry into a designated computer resource, or retrieval by the addressee if sent to a non-designated resource; or, if the addressee has not designated a computer resource along with specified timings, when the record enters the addressee's computer resource. The provisions of sub-section (2) apply notwithstanding the computer resource location differing from the deemed place of receipt, and for determining the place of business, the principal place of business is used if more than one exists, or the "usual place of residence" if no place of business exists, which for a body corporate means the place where it is registered.
children.content: 13. Time and place of despatch and receipt of electronic record. –( 1 ) Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, the despatch of an electronic record occurs when it enters a computer resource outside the control of the originator. 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signature” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 2. Ins. by s. 9, ibid . (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 3. Subs. by s. 10, ibid ., for “agreed with the addressee” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). ( 2 ) Save  as  otherwise  agreed  between the originator and  the  addressee, the  time  of  receipt of an electronic record shall be determined as follows, namely:– ( a ) if the addressee has designated a computer resource for the purpose of receiving electronic records,– ( i ) receipt occurs at the time when the electronic record enters the designated computer resource; or ( ii ) if the electronic record is sent to a computer resource of the addressee that is not the designated computer resource, receipt occurs at the time when the electronic record is retrieved by the addressee; ( b ) if the addressee has not designated a computer resource along with specified timings, if any, receipt occurs when the electronic record enters the computer resource of the addressee. ( 3 ) Save as otherwise agreed to between the originator and the addressee, an electronic record is deemed to be despatched at the place where the originator has his place of business, and is deemed to be received at the place where the addressee has his place of business. ( 4 ) The provisions of sub-section ( 2 ) shall apply notwithstanding that the place where the computer resource is located may be different from the place where the electronic record is deemed to have been received under sub-section ( 3 ). ( 5 ) For the purposes of this section,– ( a ) if the originator or the addressee has more than one place of business, the principal place of business, shall be the place of business; ( b ) if the originator or the addressee does not have a place of business, his usual place of residence shall be deemed to be the place of business; ( c ) “usual place of residence”, in relation to a body corporate, means the place where it is registered.

children.title: CHAPTER V: S ECURE ELECTRONIC RECORDS ANS SECURE 1 [ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE]
children.summary: Chapter V establishes the legal framework for "secure electronic records" and "secure electronic signatures," focusing on the application of "security procedures" from a "specific point of time" until the "time of verification." It mandates that "signature creation data," specifically the "private key of the subscriber" in digital signatures, must remain under the "exclusive control of signatory" to be deemed secure. Additionally, it empowers the Central Government to prescribe "security procedures and practices" tailored to "commercial circumstances" and the "nature of transactions."

children.title: 14. Secure electronic record
children.summary: Section 14 stipulates that an electronic record is deemed a "secure electronic record" from the "specific point of time" a "security procedure" is applied until the "time of verification." Under Section 15, an electronic signature is deemed a "secure electronic signature" if the "signature creation data" was under the "exclusive control of signatory" and no other person at the time of affixing, and was "stored and affixed in such exclusive manner as may be prescribed." The section includes an Explanation specifying that in the case of a digital signature, "signature creation data" means the "private key of the subscriber."
children.content: 14. Secure electronic record. –Where any security procedure has been applied to an electronic record at a specific point of time, then such record shall he deemed to be a secure electronic record from such point of time to the time of verification.

children.title: 15. Secure electronic signature
children.summary: An electronic signature is deemed to be a secure electronic signature if the signature creation data, at the time of affixing signature, was under the exclusive control of signatory and no other person, and was stored and affixed in such exclusive manner as may be prescribed. The section includes an Explanation stating that in the case of a digital signature, “signature creation data” means the private key of the subscriber.
children.content: 15. Secure electronic signature. – An electronic signature shall be deemed to be a secure electronic signature if– ( i ) the signature creation data, at the time of affixing signature, was under the exclusive control of signatory and no other person; and ( ii ) the signature creation data was stored and affixed in such exclusive manner as may be prescribed. Explanation. –In case of digital signature, the “signature creation data” means the private key of the subscriber.

children.title: 16. Security procedures and practices
children.summary: The Central Government may, for the purposes of sections 14 and 15, prescribe the security procedures and practices. A Proviso mandates that in prescribing such security procedures and practices, the Central Government shall have regard to the commercial circumstances, nature of transactions and such other related factors as it may consider appropriate.
children.content: 16. Security procedures and practices.– The Central Government may, for the purposes of sections 14 and 15, prescribe the security procedures and practices: Provided that in prescribing such security procedures and practices, the Central Government shall have regard to the commercial circumstances, nature of transactions and such other related factors as it may consider appropriate.]

children.title: CHAPTER VI: R EGULATION OF C ERTIFYING A UTHORITIES
children.summary: Chapter VI establishes the regulatory framework for Certifying Authorities (CAs) under the supervision of the Controller, governing the entire lifecycle of CA licensing, including application, renewal, suspension, and revocation. The scope encompasses mandatory security procedures, disclosure obligations, and compliance standards to ensure the integrity and reliability of electronic signature and Digital Signature Certificates (DSCs) issued to subscribers. Additionally, the chapter empowers the Controller with investigative authorities, including access to computer systems and data, to address contraventions and manage the public notification of certificate and license status via specified repositories.

children.title: 17. Appointment of Controller and other officers
children.summary: The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities and may also appoint Deputy Controllers, Assistant Controllers, other officers and employees. The Controller discharges functions subject to the general control and directions of the Central Government, while Deputy Controllers and Assistant Controllers perform functions assigned by the Controller under the Controller's general superintendence and control. The Central Government prescribes the qualifications, experience and terms and conditions of service, and specifies the locations for the Head Office and Branch Office of the office of the Controller, which shall have a seal.
children.content: 17. Appointment of Controller and other officers. –( 1 ) The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint a Controller of Certifying Authorities for the purposes of this Act and may also by the same or subsequent notification appoint such number of Deputy Controllers , Assistant Controllers, other officers and employees]  as it deems fit. 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signatures” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 2. Subs. by s 11, ibid., for sections 15 and 16 (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 3. Subs. by s.12, ibid., for “and Assistant Controllers” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). ( 2 ) The Controller shall discharge his functions under this Act subject to the general control and directions of the Central Government. ( 3 ) The Deputy Controllers and Assistant Controllers shall perform the functions assigned to them by the Controller under the general superintendence and control of the Controller. ( 4 ) The qualifications, experience and terms and conditions of service of Controller, Deputy Controllers 1 [, Assistant Controllers, other officers and employees] shall be such as may be prescribed by the Central Government. ( 5 ) The Head Office and Branch Office of the office of the Controller shall be at such places as the Central Government may specify, and these may be established at such places as the Central Government may think fit. ( 6 ) There shall be a seal of the Office of the Controller.

children.title: 18. Functions of Controller
children.summary: The Controller exercises supervision over Certifying Authorities, certifies their public keys, lays down standards and duties, and specifies the qualifications and experience of Certifying Authority employees and conditions for conducting business. The Controller specifies the form and content of electronic signature Certificates and keys, the contents of related materials and advertisements, and the manner of account maintenance by Certifying Authorities. Furthermore, the Controller specifies auditor appointment terms and remuneration, facilitates and regulates electronic systems established solely or jointly by Certifying Authorities, resolves conflict of interests between Certifying Authorities and subscribers, and maintains a public-accessible database of disclosure records for every Certifying Authority.
children.content: 18. Functions of Controller. –The Controller may perform all or any of the following functions, namely:– ( a ) exercising supervision over the activities of the Certifying Authorities; ( b ) certifying public keys of the Certifying Authorities; ( c ) laying down the standards to be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; ( d ) specifying the qualifications and experience which employees of the Certifying Authority should possess; ( e ) specifying the conditions subject to which the Certifying Authorities shall conduct their business; ( f ) specifying the contents of written, printed or visual materials and advertisements that may be distributed or used in respect of a 2 [electronic signature] Certificate and the public key; ( g ) specifying the form and content of a 2 [electronic signature] Certificate and the key; ( h ) specifying the form and manner in which accounts shall be maintained by the Certifying Authorities; ( i ) specifying the terms and conditions subject to which auditors may be appointed and the remuneration to be paid to them; ( j ) facilitating the establishment of any electronic system by a Certifying Authority either solely or jointly with other Certifying Authorities and regulation of such systems; ( k ) specifying the manner in which the Certifying Authorities shall conduct their dealings with the subscribers; ( l ) resolving any conflict of interests between the Certifying Authorities and the subscribers; ( m ) laying down the duties of the Certifying Authorities; ( n ) maintaining a data base containing the disclosure record of every Certifying Authority containing such particulars as may be specified by regulations, which shall be accessible to public.

children.title: 19. Recognition of foreign Certifying Authorities
children.summary: Subject to specified conditions and restrictions, the Controller may, with previous approval of the Central Government and by notification in the Official Gazette, recognise any foreign Certifying Authority as a Certifying Authority for the purposes of this Act. Any "electronic signature" Certificate issued by a Certifying Authority recognised under sub-section (1) shall be valid for the purposes of this Act. The Controller may revoke such recognition by notification in the Official Gazette, for reasons recorded in writing, if satisfied that the Certifying Authority contravened any conditions and restrictions under sub-section (1).
children.content: 19. Recognition of foreign Certifying Authorities. –( 1 ) Subject to such conditions and restrictions as may be specified by regulations, the Controller may with the previous approval of the Central Government, and by notification in the Official Gazette, recognise any foreign Certifying Authority as a Certifying Authority for the purposes of this Act. ( 2 ) Where any Certifying Authority is recognised under sub-section ( 1 ), the 2 [electronic signature] Certificate issued by such Certifying Authority shall be valid for the purposes of this Act. ( 3 ) The Controller may, if he is satisfied that any Certifying Authority has contravened any of the conditions and restrictions subject to which it was granted recognition under sub-section ( 1 ) he may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, by notification in the Official Gazette, revoke such recognition. 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 12, for “Assistant Controllers” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 2. Subs. by s. 2, ibid., for “digital signature” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009).

children.title: 20. [ Controller to act as repository. ] Omitted by the Information Technology ( Amendment ) Act , 2008 (10 of 2009), s . 13 ( w . e . f . 27
children.summary: Section 20, titled "[ Controller to act as repository. ]", has been omitted. The omission was enacted by the Information Technology ( Amendment ) Act , 2008 (10 of 2009), s . 13. This omission is effective with w . e . f . 27-10-2009.
children.content: 20. [ Controller to act as repository. ] Omitted by the Information Technology ( Amendment ) Act , 2008 (10 of 2009), s . 13 ( w . e . f . 27-10-2009).

children.title: 21. Licence to issue 1 [electronic signature] Certificates
children.summary: Subject to sub-section (2), any person may apply to the Controller for a licence to issue [electronic signature] Certificates. Under sub-section (2), no licence shall be issued unless the applicant fulfills requirements regarding qualification, expertise, manpower, financial resources, and other infrastructure facilities as prescribed by the Central Government. A licence granted under this section shall be valid for such period as may be prescribed by the Central Government, shall not be transferable or heritable, and shall be subject to terms and conditions specified by the regulations.
children.content: 21. Licence to issue 1 [electronic signature] Certificates. –( 1 ) Subject to the provisions of sub-section ( 2 ), any person may make an application, to the Controller, for a licence to issue 1 [electronic signature] Certificates. ( 2 ) No licence shall be issued under sub-section ( 1 ), unless the applicant fulfills such requirements with respect to qualification, expertise, manpower, financial resources and other infrastructure facilities, which arc necessary to issue 1 [electronic signature] Certificates as may be prescribed by the Central Government. ( 3 ) A licence granted under this section shall– ( a ) be valid for such period as may be prescribed by the Central Government; ( b ) not be transferable or heritable; ( c ) be subject to such terms and conditions as may be specified by the regulations.

children.title: 22. Application for licence
children.summary: Every application for issue of a licence shall be in such form as may be prescribed by the Central Government. Every such application shall be accompanied by a certification practice statement, a statement including the procedures with respect to identification of the applicant, payment of such fees, not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees as may be prescribed by the Central Government, and such other documents as may be prescribed by the Central Government.
children.content: 22. Application for licence. –( 1 ) Every application for issue of a licence shall be in such form as may be prescribed by the Central Government. ( 2 ) Every application for issue of a licence shall be accompanied by– ( a ) a certification practice statement; ( b ) a statement including the procedures with respect to identification of the applicant; ( c ) payment of such fees, not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees as may be prescribed by the Central Government; ( d ) such other documents, as may be prescribed by the Central Government.

children.title: 23. Renewal of licence
children.summary: An application for renewal of a licence shall be made in such form and accompanied by such fees, not exceeding five thousand rupees, as may be prescribed by the Central Government. This application must be made not less than forty-five days before the date of expiry of the period of validity of the licence.
children.content: 23. Renewal of licence. –An application for renewal of a licence shall be– ( a ) in such form; ( b ) accompanied by such fees, not exceeding five thousand rupees, as may be prescribed by the Central Government and shall be made not less than forty-five days before the date of expiry of the period of validity of the licence.

children.title: 24. Procedure for grant or rejection of licence
children.summary: Under Section 24, the Controller may, upon receipt of an application under sub-section (1) of section 21, grant the licence or reject the application after considering accompanying documents and such other factors as he deems fit. A proviso stipulates that no application shall be rejected under this section unless the applicant has been given a reasonable opportunity of presenting his case.
children.content: 24. Procedure for grant or rejection of licence. –The Controller may, on receipt of an application under sub-section ( 1 ) of section 21, after considering the documents accompanying the application and such other factors, as he deems fit, grant the licence or reject the application: Provided that no application shall be rejected under this section unless the applicant has been given a reasonable opportunity of presenting his case.

children.title: 25. Suspension of licence
children.summary: The Controller may revoke a Certifying Authority's licence if, after inquiry, the authority makes a statement in or in relation to an application for the issue or renewal of the licence that is incorrect or false in material particulars, fails to comply with the terms and conditions of the licence, fails to maintain the procedures and standards specified in section 30, or contravenes any provisions of this Act, rule, regulation or order, provided that no licence shall be revoked unless the Certifying Authority has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause. The Controller may also suspend a licence pending the completion of an enquiry if there is reasonable cause to believe grounds for revocation under sub-section (1) exist, subject to a proviso that no licence shall be suspended for a period exceeding ten days unless the Certifying Authority has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause. Per sub-section (3), no Certifying Authority whose licence has been suspended shall issue any electronic signature Certificate during such suspension.
children.content: 25. Suspension of licence. – ( 1 ) The Controller may, if he is satisfied after making such inquiry, as he may think fit, that a Certifying Authority has– ( a ) made a statement in, or in relation to, the application for the issue or renewal of the licence, which is incorrect or false in material particulars; ( b ) failed to comply with the terms and conditions subject to which the licence was granted; ( c ) failed to maintain the procedures and standards specified in section 30;] ( d ) contravened any provisions of this Act, rule, regulation or order made thereunder, revoke the licence: Provided that no licence shall be revoked unless the Certifying Authority has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause against the proposed revocation. ( 2 ) The Controller may, if he has reasonable cause to believe that there is any ground for revoking a licence under sub-section ( 1 ), by order suspend such licence pending the completion of any enquiry ordered by him: 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signature” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 2. Subs. by notification No. S.O. 1015(E) (w.e.f. 19-9-2002). Provided that no licence shall be suspended for a period exceeding ten days unless the Certifying Authority has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause against the proposed suspension. ( 3 ) No Certifying Authority whose licence has been suspended shall issue any 1 [electronic signature] Certificate during such suspension.

children.title: 26. Notice of suspension or revocation of licence
children.summary: Upon suspension or revocation of the licence of the Certifying Authority, the Controller shall publish notice of such suspension or revocation in the data base maintained by him and, where one or more repositories are specified, in all such repositories. The section contains a Proviso requiring that the data base containing the notice be made available through a website accessible round the clock. A further Proviso allows the Controller, if he considers necessary, to publicise the contents of the data base in such electronic or other media as he may consider appropriate.
children.content: 26. Notice of suspension or revocation of licence. –( 1 ) Where the licence of the Certifying Authority is suspended or revoked, the Controller shall publish notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in the data base maintained by him. ( 2 ) Where one or more repositories are specified, the Controller shall publish notices of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in all such repositories: Provided that the data base containing the notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, shall be made available through a web site which shall be accessible round the clock: Provided further that the Controller may, if he considers necessary, publicise the contents of data base in such electronic or other media, as he may consider appropriate.

children.title: 27. Power to delegate
children.summary: Section 27 provides the "Power to delegate," whereby the Controller may, in writing, authorise the Deputy Controller, Assistant Controller or any officer to exercise any of the powers of the Controller under this Chapter. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
children.content: 27. Power to delegate. –The Controller may, in writing, authorise the Deputy Controller, Assistant Controller or any officer to exercise any of the powers of the Controller under this Chapter.

children.title: 28. Power to investigate contraventions
children.summary: The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall take up for investigation any contravention of the provisions of this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder. The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall exercise the like powers which are conferred on Income-tax authorities under Chapter XIII of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961), subject to such limitations laid down under that Act.
children.content: 28. Power to investigate contraventions.– ( 1 ) The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall take up for investigation any contravention of the provisions of this Act, rules or regulations made thereunder. ( 2 ) The Controller or any officer authorised by him in this behalf shall exercise the like powers which are conferred on Income-tax authorities under Chapter XIII of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961), and shall exercise such powers, subject to such limitations laid down under that Act.

children.title: 29. Access to computers and data
children.summary: Without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 69, the Controller or any person authorised by him, upon reasonable cause to suspect any contravention of the provisions of this Chapter, shall have access to any computer system, any apparatus, data, or any other material connected with such system for the purpose of searching or causing a search to be made for obtaining information or data. For these purposes, the Controller or authorised person may, by order, direct any person in charge of, or otherwise concerned with the operation of, the computer system, data apparatus, or material to provide reasonable technical and other assistance.
children.content: 29. Access to computers and data. –( 1 ) Without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section ( 1 ) of section 69, the Controller or any person authorised by him shall, if he has reasonable cause to suspect that any contravention of the provisions of this Chapter] has been committed, have access to any computer system, any apparatus, data or any other material connected with such system, for the purpose of searching or causing a search to be made for obtaining any information or data contained in or available to such computer system. ( 2 ) For the purposes of sub-section ( 1 ), the Controller or any person authorised by him may, by order, direct any person in charge of, or otherwise concerned with the operation of, the computer system, data apparatus or material, to provide him with such reasonable technical and other assistance as he may consider necessary.

children.title: 30. Certifying Authority to follow certain procedures
children.summary: Every Certifying Authority shall make use of hardware, software and procedures secure from intrusion and misuse, provide a reasonable level of reliability in its services which are reasonably suited to the performance of intended functions, and adhere to security procedures to ensure that the secrecy and privacy of electronic signatures are assured. Such authority shall also be the repository of all electronic signature Certificates issued under this Act and publish information regarding its practices, electronic signature Certificates and current status of such certificates. Lastly, the Certifying Authority must observe such other standards as may be specified by regulations.
children.content: 30. Certifying Authority to follow certain procedures. –Every Certifying Authority shall,– ( a ) make use of hardware, software and procedures that are secure from intrusion and misuse; ( b ) provide a reasonable level of reliability in its services which are reasonably suited to the performance of intended functions; ( c ) adhere to security procedures to ensure that the secrecy and privacy of the 1 [electronic signatures] are assured; 3 *** ( ca ) be the repository of all electronic signature Certificates issued under this Act; ( cb ) publish information regarding its practices, electronic signature Certificates and current status of such certificates; and ] ( d ) observe such other standards as may be specified by regulations.

children.title: 31. Certifying Authority to ensure compliance of the Act, etc
children.summary: Section 31 mandates that every Certifying Authority ensure that every person employed or otherwise engaged by it complies with the provisions of this Act, rules, regulations and orders made thereunder. This obligation of compliance applies to such persons in the course of his employment or engagement.
children.content: 31. Certifying Authority to ensure compliance of the Act, etc. –Every Certifying Authority shall ensure that every person employed or otherwise engaged by it complies, in the course of his employment or engagement, with the provisions of this Act, rules, regulations and orders made thereunder. 1. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signature” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 2. Subs. by s. 14, ibid., for “any contravention of the provisions of this Act, rules and regulations made thereunder” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 3. The word “and” omitted by s. 15, ibid. (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 4. Ins. by s. 15, ibid . (w.e.f. 27-10-2009).

children.title: 32. Display of licence
children.summary: Under Section 32, every Certifying Authority shall display its licence. This display must be located at a conspicuous place of the premises in which the Certifying Authority carries on its business.
children.content: 32. Display of licence. –Every Certifying Authority shall display its licence at a conspicuous place of the premises in which it carries on its business.

children.title: 33. Surrender of licence
children.summary: Under Section 33(1), every Certifying Authority whose licence is suspended or revoked shall immediately surrender the licence to the Controller. Pursuant to Section 33(2), failure to surrender a licence renders the person in whose favour a licence is issued guilty of an offence, liable to a penalty which may extend to five lakh rupees.
children.content: 33. Surrender of licence. –( 1 ) Every Certifying Authority whose licence is suspended or revoked shall immediately after such suspension or revocation, surrender the licence to the Controller. ( 2 ) Where any Certifying Authority fails to surrender a licence under sub-section ( 1 ), the person in whose favour a licence is issued, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be 1 [liable to penalty which may extend to five lakh rupees].

children.title: 34. Disclosure
children.summary: Every Certifying Authority shall disclose in the manner specified by regulations its [electronic signature] Certificate, any relevant certification practice statement, notice of the revocation or suspension of its Certifying Authority certificate, and any fact that materially and adversely affects either the reliability of an issued [electronic signature] Certificate or the Authority's ability to perform its services. If any event or situation arises which may materially and adversely affect the integrity of its computer system or the conditions subject to which a [electronic signature] Certificate was granted, the Certifying Authority shall use reasonable efforts to notify any person likely to be affected or act in accordance with the procedure specified in its certification practice statement.
children.content: 34. Disclosure. –( 1 ) Every Certifying Authority shall disclose in the manner specified by regulations– ( a ) its 2 [electronic signature] Certificate 3 ***; ( b ) any certification practice statement relevant thereto; ( c ) notice of the revocation or suspension of its Certifying Authority certificate, if any; and ( d ) any other fact that materially and adversely affects either the reliability of a 2 [electronic signature] Certificate, which that Authority has issued, or the Authority's ability to perform its services. ( 2 ) Where in the opinion of the Certifying Authority any event has occurred or any situation has arisen which may materially and adversely affect the integrity of its computer system or the conditions subject to which a 2 [electronic signature] Certificate was granted, then, the Certifying Authority shall– ( a ) use reasonable efforts to notify any person who is likely to be affected by that occurrence; or ( b ) act in accordance with the procedure specified in its certification practice statement to deal with such event or situation. CHAPTERVII E S ] C LECTRONIC IGNATURE ERTIFICATES

children.title: 35. Certifying authority to issue 2 [electronic signature] Certificate
children.summary: Any person may apply to the Certifying Authority for an "electronic signature" Certificate using a form prescribed by the Central Government, accompanied by a fee not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees, subject to a Proviso allowing different fees for different classes of applicants. Each application must include a certification practice statement or a statement containing particulars specified by regulations. The Certifying Authority may grant the "electronic signature" Certificate or reject the application for reasons recorded in writing after conducting necessary enquiries, provided that a Proviso mandates no rejection occur unless the applicant has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause.
children.content: 35. Certifying authority to issue 2 [electronic signature] Certificate.– ( 1 ) Any person may make an application to the Certifying Authority for the issue of a 2 [electronic signature] Certificate in such form as may be prescribed by the Central Government. ( 2 ) Every such application shall be accompanied by such fee not exceeding twenty-five thousand rupees as may be prescribed by the Central Government, to be paid to the Certifying Authority: Provided that while prescribing fees under sub-section ( 2 ) different fees may be prescribed for different classes of applicants. ( 3 ) Every such application shall be accompanied by a certification practice statement or where there is no such statement, a statement containing such particulars, as may be specified by regulations. ( 4 ) On receipt of an application under sub-section ( 1 ), the Certifying Authority may, after consideration of the certification practice statement or the other statement under sub-section ( 3 ) and after making such enquiries as it may deem fit, grant the 2 [electronic signature] Certificate or for reasons to be recorded in writing, reject the application: 4 * * * * * Provided] that no application shall be rejected unless the applicant has been given a reasonable opportunity of showing cause against the proposed rejection.

children.title: 36. Representations upon issuance of Digital Signature Certificate
children.summary: Under Section 36, a Certifying Authority issuing a Digital Signature Certificate shall certify compliance with the provisions of the Act, rules, and regulations, and that the Digital Signature Certificate was published or made available to the relying person and accepted by the subscriber. The Certifying Authority must certify that the subscriber holds a private key corresponding to the listed public key, a private key capable of creating a digital signature, and that the public and private keys constitute a functioning key pair where the public key verifies a digital signature affixed by the private key. Additionally, it must certify that the information in the Digital Signature Certificate is accurate and that the Certifying Authority has no knowledge of any material fact which would adversely affect the reliability of the representations in clauses (a) to (d).
children.content: 36. Representations upon issuance of Digital Signature Certificate. –A Certifying Authority while issuing a Digital Signature Certificate shall certify that– ( a ) it has complied with the provisions of this Act and the rules and regulations made thereunder; ( b ) it has published the Digital Signature Certificate or otherwise made it available to such person relying on it and the subscriber has accepted it; 1. Subs. by Act 18 of 2023, s. 2 and Schedule for certain words (w.e.f. 30-11-2023). 2. Subs. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 2, for “digital signature” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 3. Certain words omitted by s. 16, ibid . (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 4. The first proviso omitted by s. 17, ibid . (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). 5. Subs. by s. 17, ibid ., for “Provided further” (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). ( c ) the subscriber holds the private key corresponding to the public key, listed in the Digital Signature Certificate; ( ca ) the subscriber holds a private key which is capable of creating a digital signature; ( cb ) the public key to be listed in the certificate can be used to verify a digital signature affixed by the private key held by the subscriber;] ( d ) the subscriber's public key and private key constitute a functioning key pair; ( e ) the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate is accurate; and ( f ) it has no knowledge of any material fact, which if it had been included in the Digital Signature Certificate would adversely affect the reliability of the representations in clauses ( a ) to ( d ).

children.title: 37. Suspension of Digital Signature Certificate
children.summary: Under Section 37(1), the Certifying Authority may suspend a Digital Signature Certificate upon request from the subscriber or any person duly authorised to act on behalf of that subscriber, or if the Certifying Authority is of the opinion that suspension is in public interest. Pursuant to sub-section (2), a Digital Signature Certificate shall not be suspended for a period exceeding fifteen days unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard. Per sub-section (3), the Certifying Authority must communicate the suspension to the subscriber.
children.content: 37. Suspension of Digital Signature Certificate. –( 1 ) Subject to the provisions of sub-section ( 2 ), the Certifying Authority which has issued a Digital Signature Certificate may suspend such Digital Signature Certificate,– ( a ) on receipt of a request to that effect from– ( i ) the subscriber listed in the Digital Signature Certificate; or ( ii ) any person duly authorised to act on behalf of that subscriber; ( b ) if it is of opinion that the Digital Signature Certificate should be suspended in public interest. ( 2 ) A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be suspended for a period exceeding fifteen days unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard in the matter. ( 3 ) On suspension of a Digital Signature Certificate under this section, the Certifying Authority shall communicate the same to the subscriber.

children.title: 38. Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate
children.summary: A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate upon request by the subscriber or an authorized person, upon the death of the subscriber, or upon the dissolution of the firm or winding up of the company. Without prejudice to sub-section (1), revocation may occur if a material fact represented in the Digital Signature Certificate is false or has been concealed, a requirement for issuance was not satisfied, the Certifying Authority's private key or security system was compromised in a manner materially affecting the Digital Signature Certificate's reliability, or the subscriber is declared insolvent, dead, or the firm/company has been dissolved, wound-up, or otherwise ceased to exist. Subject to the provision that a Digital Signature Certificate shall not be revoked unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard, the Certifying Authority shall communicate the revocation to the subscriber.
children.content: 38. Revocation of Digital Signature Certificate. –( 1 ) A Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate issued by it– ( a ) where the subscriber or any other person authorised by him makes a request to that effect; or ( b ) upon the death of the subscriber; or ( c ) upon the dissolution of the firm or winding up of the company where the subscriber is a firm or a company. ( 2 ) Subject to the provisions of sub-section ( 3 ) and without prejudice to the provisions of sub-section ( 1 ), a Certifying Authority may revoke a Digital Signature Certificate which has been issued by it at any time, if it is of opinion that– ( a ) a material fact represented in the Digital Signature Certificate is false or has been concealed; ( b ) a requirement for issuance of the Digital Signature Certificate was not satisfied; ( c ) the Certifying Authority's private key or security system was compromised in a manner materially affecting the Digital Signature Certificate's reliability; ( d ) the subscriber has been declared insolvent or dead or where a subscriber is a firm or a company, which has been dissolved, wound-up or otherwise ceased to exist. ( 3 ) A Digital Signature Certificate shall not be revoked unless the subscriber has been given an opportunity of being heard in the matter. ( 4 ) On revocation of a Digital Signature Certificate under this section, the Certifying Authority shall communicate the same to the subscriber.

children.title: 39. Notice of suspension or revocation
children.summary: Upon suspension or revocation of a Digital Signature Certificate under section 37 or section 38, the Certifying Authority shall publish a notice of such suspension or revocation in the repository specified in the Digital Signature Certificate. Where one or more repositories are specified, the Certifying Authority shall publish notices of such suspension or revocation in all such repositories.
children.content: 39. Notice of suspension or revocation. –( 1 ) Where a Digital Signature Certificate is suspended or revoked under section 37 or section 38, the Certifying Authority shall publish a notice of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in the repository specified in the Digital Signature Certificate for publication of such notice. 1. Ins. by Act 10 of 2009, s. 18 (w.e.f. 27-10-2009). ( 2 ) Where one or more repositories are specified, the Certifying Authority shall publish notices of such suspension or revocation, as the case may be, in all such repositories.

children.title: CHAPTER VIII: D UTIES OF SUBSCRIBERS
children.summary: Chapter VIII regulates the legal obligations of subscribers regarding the generation, acceptance, and management of Digital Signature Certificates (DSC) and Electronic Signature Certificates. It mandates adherence to security procedures for key pair generation and establishes that the acceptance of a certificate constitutes a legal certification of truthfulness regarding representations made to the Certifying Authority. Furthermore, the chapter imposes a duty of reasonable care for private key control, requiring immediate notification of any compromise to the Certifying Authority to terminate the subscriber's liability.

children.title: 40. Generating key pair
children.summary: Section 40 mandates that where a subscriber accepts a Digital Signature Certificate containing a public key that corresponds to the subscriber's private key, the subscriber shall generate that key pair by applying the security procedure. Section 40A further stipulates that in respect of an Electronic Signature Certificate, the subscriber shall perform such duties as may be prescribed.
children.content: 40. Generating key pair. –Where any Digital Signature Certificate the public key of which corresponds to the private key of that subscriber which is to be listed in the Digital Signature Certificate has been accepted by a subscriber, 1 *** the subscriber shall generate 2 [that key] pair by applying the security procedure.

children.title: 40A. Duties of subscriber of Electronic Signature Certificate
children.summary: Section 40A mandates the duties of the subscriber of an Electronic Signature Certificate. It stipulates that in respect of an Electronic Signature Certificate, the subscriber shall perform such duties as may be prescribed.
children.content: 40A. Duties of subscriber of Electronic Signature Certificate.– In respect of Electronic Signature Certificate the subscriber shall perform such duties as may be prescribed.]

children.title: 41. Acceptance of Digital Signature Certificate
children.summary: A subscriber shall be deemed to have accepted a Digital Signature Certificate if they publish or authorise the publication of a Digital Signature Certificate to one or more persons, in a repository, or otherwise demonstrate approval in any manner. By accepting, the subscriber certifies to all who reasonably rely on the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate that the subscriber holds the private key corresponding to the public key listed and is entitled to hold the same. Furthermore, the subscriber certifies that all representations made to the Certifying Authority and all material relevant to the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate are true, and all information in the Digital Signature Certificate that is within the knowledge of the subscriber is true.
children.content: 41. Acceptance of Digital Signature Certificate. –( 1 ) A subscriber shall be deemed to have accepted a Digital Signature Certificate if he publishes or authorises the publication of a Digital Signature Certificate– ( a ) to one or more persons; ( b ) in a repository; or otherwise demonstrates his approval of the Digital Signature Certificate in any manner. ( 2 ) By accepting a Digital Signature Certificate the subscriber certifies to all who reasonably rely on the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate that– ( a ) the subscriber holds the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate and is entitled to hold the same; ( b ) all representations made by the subscriber to the Certifying Authority and all material relevant to the information contained in the Digital Signature Certificate are true; ( c ) all information in the Digital Signature Certificate that is within the knowledge of the subscriber is true.

children.title: 42. Control of private key
children.summary: Every subscriber shall exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in his Digital Signature Certificate and take all steps to prevent its disclosure. If the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate has been compromised, the subscriber shall communicate the same without any delay to the Certifying Authority in such manner as may be specified by the regulations. An Explanation clarifies that the subscriber shall be liable till he has informed the Certifying Authority that the private key has been compromised.
children.content: 42. Control of private key.– ( 1 ) Every subscriber shall exercise reasonable care to retain control of the private key corresponding to the public key listed in his Digital Signature Certificate and take all steps to prevent its disclosure 4 ***. ( 2 ) If the private key corresponding to the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate has been compromised, then, the subscriber shall communicate the same without any delay to the Certifying Authority in such manner as may be specified by the regulations. Explanation .–For the removal of doubts, it is hereby declared that the subscriber shall be liable till he has informed the Certifying Authority that the private key has been compromised. ENALTIES, COMPENSATION AND ADJUDICATION

children.title: CHAPTER IX: 5 [P ]
children.summary: Chapter IX establishes the framework for civil liability, prescribing penalties and compensation for unauthorized access, data manipulation, introduction of computer contaminants, and negligence by bodies corporate in maintaining reasonable security practices for sensitive personal data or information. It further addresses penalties for the failure to furnish information or maintain required records, alongside a residuary penalty for unspecified contraventions of the Act's rules, regulations, or directions. To enforce these provisions, the chapter mandates the appointment of an Adjudicating Officer, vested with civil court powers, to adjudicate contraventions and determine the quantum of compensation by evaluating the loss caused, unfair advantage gained, and the repetitive nature of the default.

children.title: 43. 6 [Penalty and compensation] for damage to computer, computer system, etc
children.summary: Section 43 mandates that any person who, without permission of the owner or person in charge, accesses/secures access to, downloads/copies/extracts data/computer data base/information from, introduces a computer contaminant/computer virus into, damages, disrupts, denies access to, facilitates unauthorized access to, manipulates accounts of, or destroys/alters information or computer source code within a computer, computer system, computer network, or computer resource, is liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected, per an Explanation defining "computer contaminant", "computer data-base", "computer virus", "damage", and "computer source code". Section 43A stipulates that a "body corporate" possessing, dealing or handling "sensitive personal data or information" in a "computer resource" that is negligent in implementing and maintaining "reasonable security practices and procedures", thereby causing "wrongful loss or wrongful gain" to any person, shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected, as clarified by an Explanation defining "body corporate", "reasonable security practices and procedures", and "sensitive personal data or information".
